explanation:
First, the requirement applies only in PCV and PSV modes, which means (PCVMode | PSVMode) guards the rest of the property. The property states that there shall be a possibility to press a button (expiratoryPauseButton), which when held, initiates an expiratory pause (expiratoryPause) that closes both inspiratory and expiratory valves (inValveClose & outValveClose) at the end of the expiration phase (expiratoryPhaseEnd), as long as the operator holds the button, but no longer than 60 seconds.

Given real-time is not directly expressible in LTL, the 'no longer than 60 sec' part can only be represented abstractly, i.e., by a formula stating that for as long as the button is held, the pause/valve closure holds, but there exists a bound (not directly formalizable in pure LTL) when releasing is forced. We'll focus on the core safety property, not the explicit timeout.

Key steps:
- (PCVMode | PSVMode): only relevant in these modes.
- If, after expiratory phase ends, the operator presses and holds expiratoryPauseButton, then, at the next step, an expiratory pause begins (expiratoryPause is true), and both inValve and outValve are closed, and these stay true as long as the button is held.
- When the button is released or after 60 seconds, the pause ends. We'll only model the button hold and ignore explicit timing.

Core LTL property: In PCV or PSV mode, whenever expiratoryPhaseEnd occurs and the operator presses expiratoryPauseButton, then, starting next, for as long as expiratoryPauseButton is held and not more than 60s (not expressible), expiratoryPause, inValveClose, and outValveClose hold. We can therefore express: Globally, if in PCV or PSV mode, and after expiratoryPhaseEnd, expiratoryPauseButton is pressed, then, from the next step, as long as the button is pressed, expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose hold.

explanation_dict.natural_language: PCV and PSV mode
button to initiate Expiratory Pause is pressed
Expiratory Pause is active
both inspiratory and expiratory valve are closed
at the end of the expiration phase
as long as the operator holds the button
explanation_dict.LTL: as long as expiratoryPauseButton is true

output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPhaseEnd & X expiratoryPauseButton) -> X G((expiratoryPauseButton) -> (expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose)))

explanation:
The requirement states: In PCV and PSV mode, there shall be the possibility to press a single button to initiate an Expiratory Pause that closes both inspiratory and expiratory valve at the end of the expiration phase as long as the operator holds the button but no longer than 60 sec.

First, we note that this is a liveness property about providing a possibility in two modes (PCVMode OR PSVMode). When in these modes, at the end of the expiratory phase (expiratoryPhaseEnd), if the operator holds the expiratoryPauseButton, this should trigger (initiate) an expiratoryPause, which involves inValveClose AND outValveClose, for up to 60 seconds (we can only represent 'no longer than 60 sec' abstractly, as LTL itself does not encode time bounds directly; we use a generic temporal expression for this demo).

We'll focus on the part: whenever (globally) we are in PCVMode OR PSVMode, at the end of expiration, if the expiratoryPauseButton is held by the operator, then for as long as the button is held (but no more than 60 sec), an expiratoryPause is active (including inValveClose and outValveClose).

Abstracting the time bound, the formula in LTL is:
G((PCVMode | PSVMode) -> (expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton -> X F (expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose)))

This says: Whenever in PCV or PSV mode and the expiration phase ends and the button is pressed, eventually on the next steps an expiratory pause state (with valves closed) will be reached and remain as long as the button is held (the max. 60 sec not expressible directly).

explanation_dict.natural_language: In PCV and PSV mode
at the end of expiration phase
operator holds expiratory pause button
expiratory pause is active with both valves closed
As long as button held (time bounds abstracted)
Globally, if in PCV OR PSV and expiration ends, and button held, then in the next step it is possible to eventually have expiratory pause with both valves closed (while button held)
explanation_dict.LTL: G((PCVMode | PSVMode) -> (expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton -> X F (expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose)))

output_LTL: G((PCVMode | PSVMode) -> (expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton -> X F (expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose)))

explanation:
1. We first map the relevant atomic propositions: 'PCVMode' and 'PSVMode' mean the ventilator is in PCV or PSV mode, 'expiratoryPauseButton' is the single button pressed to initiate Expiratory Pause, 'expiratoryPause' is true during an expiratory pause, 'expiratoryPhaseEnd' marks the end of expiration, 'expiratoryPhase' is true during expiration, 'inValveClose' and 'outValveClose' mean both valves are closed, 'expiratoryPauseButton' remains held by the operator. 
2. The main requirement: whenever we are in PCV or PSV mode, there is the possibility to press a button ('expiratoryPauseButton') at the end of expiration ('expiratoryPhaseEnd'), and as long as it's held (up to 60 secs), 'expiratoryPause' (both valves are closed) is true. It stops if the button is released or 60 sec elapse.
3. For LTL: We need to express for all times in PCV or PSV mode and when expiration finishes, if the button is pressed, then for up to 60 steps (assuming one step is one second) while the button is continuously held, 'expiratoryPause' and the valves are both closed continuously. 
4. Use implication from 'expiratoryPauseButton' press at 'expiratoryPhaseEnd', then for the next up to 60 steps, as long as the button is held, 'expiratoryPause' & 'inValveClose' & 'outValveClose' hold. The behavior will terminate earlier if the button is released.
5. We use a bounded invariant: for all k in [1,60], if the button remains held continuously for k steps, then 'expiratoryPause', 'inValveClose', and 'outValveClose' hold for those k steps. 
6. As LTL doesn't directly support counting, we approximate with a universal form: whenever the button is being held (after it's initially pressed at the end of expiration), for up to 60 steps, the conditions hold. This is best expressed as: 
G((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton -> ((expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose) U ( (!expiratoryPauseButton) | F^{<=60}(true) ))) 
But LTL does not support explicit timing operator F^{<=60}, so typically for specs, it's written as: 
Globally, if at the end of expiratory phase in PCV or PSV mode the button is pressed, then as long as the button is held but no longer than 60 steps, expiratoryPause, inValveClose, and outValveClose hold. If the button is released or 60 are up, this must stop.
A common way is to use a safety property: 'If the button is being held after the press, for the next 60 steps these conditions hold.' 
Here we use formulas like: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPhaseEnd & X(expiratoryPauseButton)) -> ( (expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose) U ((!expiratoryPauseButton) | X^{60}(!expiratoryPauseButton)) )) 
But LTL is not able to express the 'no longer than 60 sec' precisely without extending the logic; in standard LTL, this is typically left as part of the system, or a bounded variant is written for model checking. In unbounded LTL, omit the strict timeout:
G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPhaseEnd & X(expiratoryPauseButton)) -> ((expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose) U (!expiratoryPauseButton)))

explanation_dict.natural_language: PCV or PSV mode is active
Expiratory phase ends
Operator presses the button to initiate Expiratory Pause at end of expiration
As long as the button is being held, but no longer than 60 sec, expiratoryPause holds
Globally, whenever these conditions are met, the above must happen

output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPhaseEnd & X(expiratoryPauseButton)) -> ((expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose) U (!expiratoryPauseButton)))

explanation:
We need to translate a complex requirement: When the ventilator is in PCV or PSV mode, as long as the operator holds the (expiratory pause) button, at the end of expiration phase, the inspiratory and expiratory valves remain closed, but never for longer than 60 seconds. This means: (1) Only in PCVMode or PSVMode, (2) If operator holds expiratoryPauseButton at the end of the expiration phase (expiratoryPhaseEnd), then (3) inValveClose and outValveClose both hold (valves closed), (4) but the pause cannot stay active for more than 60 sec.

Step-by-step logic:
1) The condition is only for PCV or PSV mode: (PCVMode | PSVMode)
2) The action is triggered when the operator holds the button at the end of expiration: (expiratoryPauseButton & expiratoryPhaseEnd)
3) As long as the button remains held (and not more than 60s), the valves remain closed (inValveClose & outValveClose).
4) The action must stop after 60 seconds even if the button is maintained. In LTL with discrete time, we approximate the 60 sec constraint with X-operators (if each transition is 1 sec, then X^60).

So, for each occurrence of (PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton & expiratoryPhaseEnd, inValveClose & outValveClose must hold until the first of (the button is released OR 60 steps elapse).

This can be translated as: For all time, if in PCV or PSV mode, and the pause button is pressed at end of expiration, then for up to 60 steps (or less if button released), the valves stay closed.

Because pure LTL cannot count exact steps, we can indicate the property as: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton & expiratoryPhaseEnd) -> ((inValveClose & outValveClose) U ((!expiratoryPauseButton) | F_{<=60}(!expiratoryPauseButton)))

But with basic LTL, we cannot write F_{<=60}, so for this translation, we state: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton & expiratoryPhaseEnd) -> ((inValveClose & outValveClose) U ((!expiratoryPauseButton) | X^60 true)))

But since 'no longer than 60 sec' cannot be done with pure LTL, we can leave it as:
G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton & expiratoryPhaseEnd) -> ((inValveClose & outValveClose) U (!expiratoryPauseButton)))
or, noting the 60s as an informal comment.

Explanation dict below describes main steps.

operator holds the expiratoryPauseButton at the end of expiration phase
as long as the button is held (and not more than 60s), both valves are closed
Globally for any such event

output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton & expiratoryPhaseEnd) -> ((inValveClose & outValveClose) U (!expiratoryPauseButton)))

explanation:
The requirement is: In PCV and PSV mode, there must be a button that can be pressed to initiate an Expiratory Pause which closes both the inspiratory (inValveClose) and expiratory (outValveClose) valves at the end of expiration (expiratoryPhaseEnd), as long as the button is held but no longer than 60 seconds.

Translation step by step:
1. 'In PCV and PSV mode' → (PCVMode | PSVMode) must be active, so use condition (PCVMode | PSVMode)
2. 'there shall be the possibility to press a single button to initiate an Expiratory Pause' → when the button (expiratoryPauseButton) is pressed, it is possible to enter Expiratory Pause (expiratoryPause)
3. During Expiratory Pause:
    - Inspiratory valve and expiratory valve are both closed (inValveClose & outValveClose)
    - Pause starts at the end of expiration (expiratoryPhaseEnd)
    - Pause as long as button is held (expiratoryPauseButton) but not more than 60 seconds (can be modeled as 'up to 60s', but in LTL time is abstract, so we use a time-bounded finally operator, written as F<=60s, but standard LTL doesn't support this natively; we can only say 'eventually' unless we use extensions)

As strict LTL does not support exact time bounds, a typical abstraction would be:
- Whenever the system is in PCV or PSV mode, whenever the button is pressed at the end of expiration, then (1) Expiratory Pause is initiated, (2) while the button is held, and for at most 60 sec, the valves remain closed.

We use the following atomic propositions:
- PCVMode, PSVMode: mode indicators
- expiratoryPauseButton: indicates button pressed
- expiratoryPhaseEnd: at the end of expiration
- expiratoryPause: Expiratory Pause is ongoing
- inValveClose: inspiratory valve is closed
- outValveClose: expiratory valve is closed

LTL limitations:
- For time-bounded ("but no longer than 60 sec"), we describe that "if the button is continuously pressed, the state may remain for a bounded time". In untimed LTL, we can only check 'while the button is pressed'. Time boundedness must be validated at the implementational level.

Final LTL formula:
Globally, if (PCVMode or PSVMode) and the Expiratory Pause button is pressed at the end of expiration, then an Expiratory Pause is initiated, and as long as the button is held (or for at most 60 seconds), both valves are closed.

G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton & expiratoryPhaseEnd) -> (expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose U (!expiratoryPauseButton | F expiratoryPauseButton = false)))

Since there is no direct 60s bound, we model: during Expiratory Pause (which starts at the end of expiration and button press), valves stay closed while button is pressed; after button release, pause ends and valves can reopen.

Here is the step-by-step breakdown.

Expiratory Pause is initiated and valves are closed as long as button is held (or until 60s, which cannot be exactly expressed in standard LTL)
If above conditions, then action happens

LTL cannot count steps or measure time directly, and no atomic proposition given represents time or timeout. Thus, using the allowed atomic propositions, we encode: while the operator continues to hold the Expiratory Pause button, the valves must remain closed and pause active, but we cannot enforce the 60-second bound in the logic. Globally: if in PCVMode or PSVMode, at the end of the expiration phase, user is operator, and the Expiratory Pause button is pressed, then from the next moment onward, as long as the button is held, expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose must hold. When the button is released, the requirement ends.

explanation: This requirement says: In PCV and PSV modes, it must be possible to press a single button for Expiratory Pause, which closes both inspiratory and expiratory valve at the end of the expiration phase and remains on as long as the operator holds the button but no longer than 60 seconds. Let's focus on the essence using the available atomic propositions. We will define: (1) 'In PCV or PSV mode' is PCVMode | PSVMode. (2) 'ExpiratoryPauseButton is pressed' is expiratoryPauseButton. (3) 'Expiratory Pause is active' is expiratoryPause. (4) 'At end of expiratoryPhase' is G(expiratoryPhase -> ...). (5) 'both inValveClose & outValveClose' means inValveClose & outValveClose. (6) 'as long as the button is pressed' means (expiratoryPause <-> expiratoryPauseButton) within 60 seconds. So: Whenever we are in PCV or PSV mode, at the end of expiration phase, pressing the expiratoryPauseButton causes (for at most 60s) the system to stay in expiratoryPause, and both inValve & outValve are closed as long as the button is held. To encode the maximal length, we do not have a timer proposition, so we note this as: (PCVMode | PSVMode) -> G( (expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton) -> ((expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose) U ( (!expiratoryPauseButton) | F_{≤60s}(...) ) ) ). However, classic LTL does not allow bounded time, so we note the bound in the explanation, and encode only the unbounded version. So the LTL will be: G( (PCVMode | PSVMode) -> G( (expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton) -> ( (expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose) U !expiratoryPauseButton ) ) )

explanation_dict.natural_language: In PCV or PSV mode
At the end of expiration phase
Expiratory Pause button is pressed
Expiratory Pause is active
Inspiratory & expiratory valves are closed
Pause is held as long as button is pressed
Globally, in PCV or PSV, at end of expiration if the button is pressed, Expiratory Pause and both valves stay closed until button is released
explanation_dict.LTL: G((PCVMode | PSVMode) -> G((expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton) -> ((expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose) U !expiratoryPauseButton)))
